BEARING 315
The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest
September, 1994

Scottish Highlands Host Veterans

by Margie Bone

Three Seattle orienteers, Cascade members Anne York, Harvey Freidman and Margie Bone participated in the Veterans World Cup competition in Aviemore, Scotland August 1-5. First let me clarify that this has nothing to do with either the military or with amount of orienteering experience; a "veteran" is simply someone over 35 years of age. There were 3000 participants in the VWC and then additional participants, including Eric and Nicholas Bone and Margie's parents, on "colour-coded" courses with day of event registration for those not in the Veteran's Cup.

About fifteen people from the US were there, including Sherry Litasi who will direct the VWC event in Minneapolis in 1997. There were strong performances by Al Smith of A&E Orienteering Supply fame and by Penny DeMoss from Bay Area Orienteering Club. Penny placed 10th out of about 80 women in the W40 category. It turns out Penny and her husband Harold spend about 4 months each year living in Scotland, but she still calls BAOC her home club.

The terrain was varied, with both deciduous and evergreen forests, open grazing land and large areas of heather. I had thought of heather as a soft, small plant, but lovely as it is, it is a bush to be reckoned with. It is difficult to run through, particularly for shorter people on whom the heather came to their waists. (We didn't run through any but there are some areas where the heather is burned each year because a particular type of grouse lives primarily on new shoots of heather.) Many of the evergreen areas were farmed so there were irrigation ditches between the rows of trees, and running was a lot easier along the rows than across (this I learned when my route required my going several hundred meters across).

The courses were longer than what I am used to running and there were long walks to the starts some days. Qualifying days for the 40-44 year old women were about 5 kms with up to a 3 km walk to the start. The final day was 7.2 km with a 2.5 km walk to the start. No ladders though! (I understand that participants had to climb a ladder to get to the start for one of the courses in the Scottish 6-Day last year.)

Many people found qualifying day two to be the most difficult with two distinctly different types of terrain on a single map. One part had fairly large features, then the last half of the course was in a wooded area with an incredibly complex grouping of small hills and depressions. I was being kind of casual about choosing clearly identifiable attack points because the earlier part of the course had allowed successful use of compass and pace, sometimes for entire legs. Then I got into an area where all the hills and all the depressions were about the same size and shape as the others and it was hilly and brushy enough that pace counting was difficult. There were areas with lots of competitors milling around, asking people if they knew where we were. It was a good reminder to me to plan each leg in a disciplined way and to try to keep all my orienteering techniques in mind for each leg; the course setter did a wonderful job of tapping all those skills.

It was a beautiful area (except for the town of Aviemore itself which is very touristy) with good bed and breakfasts, miles and miles of stone fences, impressive castles (it's awe inspiring to explore buildings built in the 1300's and to see the room where Mary Queen of Scots gave birth to King James the VIth), friendly people and challenging orienteering. The same Strathspey area will be the site of the Scottish 6-Day next year. If anyone would like information about that, please feel free to ask me.

3 Multi-Day Meets In A Month

by Harvey Friedman

In case any of you wondered why Anne York & Harvey Friedman didn't attend any local meets from 15 July to 15 August 1994, we were orienteering at multi-day international events in Europe. 19-23 July we did Nordvest Galoppen '94 near Nordfjordeid and Stryn, Norway. 31 July—5 August we did Veterans' World Cup near Aviemore, Scotland. 7 August we did a 1-day event at Blakeley Raise in the Lakes District, England. 12-14 August we were at the Drammen 3-day, outside of Drammen, Norway.
As those of you who have traveled in Europe well realize, hopping back and forth between Great Britain and Norway can be quite expensive. It is, but we didn't do it just for the terrific international competition. Orienteering is the true family sport suitable for all ages in these big European meets and for 10 years old and up everywhere else I've tried it. More than that, orienteering is still a sport where all participants are part of the orienteering family and big meets are one way to get together with distant "O' cousins".

In particular, Anne & I and the Bone family were met at the airport by Magne & Else Raadal (who were here last September-December ) in Bergen, Norway and helped with finding our rental car and a cash machine so we could be on our way north for NVG '94. The meet had about 700 people from about 12 different countries with most coming from western Norway. The format was run 2 days, 1 rest day, and run 2 more days with points being computed each run day. Total points for the best 3 out of 4 days determined the placings for each class. Of the 5 of us, only Eric Bone did consistently well. After the event, we drove back to Bergen through some spectacular scenery and took an hour long ferry ride so that the Bones could see even more and finally returned to the Raadal home for well-needed R&R. While in Bergen, Anne & I visited Stein Sandven, who some of you might remember from the 1981-1982 years. Stein has married Hanne Sagen, a graduate student working at Nansen Remote Sensing Center, and they have a 2-year-old son, Haakon. Buying and trying to fix up and older home and Haakon have curtailed Stein's O' activities recently but he expects to be back in 3 years. While at the Raadal's, Eric, Margie, and I competed in a 5 K fun run. Eric placed 7th overall and I racewalked it in about 35 minutes. Not bad for not having trained in RW in over a year.

The Bones left for Great Britain, where they would meet Margie's parents and drive up to Scotland and Anne & I had another relaxing day in Bergen. When we flew in to Newcastle, we had the misfortune of being in the midst of the BritRail strike, so our plan to take the train directly to Aviemore was put on hold for 2 days. (Anyone planning to get a BritRail pass or Flexipass, ask your agent about strikes. Rail strikes can play havoc with ones travel plans and budget.) We finally got to Aviemore where we were met at the rail station by David Booth & Jean Smith, a couple of British orienteers who we had met and befriended last summer at the Hamlin Park meet. We shared a wonderful, refurbished, stone cottage with them during VWC. It had a modern kitchen, 2 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms, etc.

VWC94 itself was on superb maps of intricate terrain with over 3000 competitors from more than 30 countries. What we hadn't quite realized when we had signed up for this event was that it is the age class world championships for those over 35. Consequently, many former world champions were there and overall these were the fittest, most successful orienteers in the world. As you might have guessed, those of us who were below average in rankings in the US didn't do very well against the best in the world.

One somewhat sad note to relate. One of the Swedish women who had been in O' for over 50 years and who ran in W75 died on her way to the 1st control on the final day. At first, we were dismayed but those people who had known her best said that many times she expressed the following sentiments: —I do not want to die of Alzheimer's disease. I do not want to die as a vegetable in a nursing home. I do not want to be sick and waste away in pain. If I am to die, I would prefer it to be running through the woods doing something I've loved for years.— So the rest of us all felt better thinking that she had died happily.

After VWC94, Anne & I drove down to Ulverston with Jean & David and went to a "color-coded event" in the Lakes District. In the moor lands I had my best run of the vacation, doing a 3.8 K course in under 56 minutes. It was similar to Marymoor Park in visibility and openness, but more like Cle Elum Ridge in steepness.

After spending a day being tourists in Ulverston, home of Swarthmoor Hall, where the Society of Friends (Quakers) was founded, we departed by train for Heathrow Airport, London and our flight to Oslo. Ingemar J. Haverstad, who some of you might remember when he was Ingemar Jansson, met us at the airport and took us home to Helga and the 3 kids. They have a wonderful house in Tranby on the edge of some wonderful hills with steep cliffs and full of cows, sheep, goats, berries, etc. Naturally, there is O' map of it. Anne, guided by the 6 year-old Sindre, took him and 4 year-old Eirik, up the hill to pick blueberries. Using the Scandinavian 'plokker', one of which Anee brought home, they picked about 5 liters in 2 hours. Anne introduced home-made blueberry pie to the gang and everyone wanted more. The next day we combined a hike, lake stay, O' training, with more blueberry picking and thus more pie. The 1st day of the 3-day had a mass start at 1800 to 1930, with 10 minutes separating each of the classes. In this way, folks could get 3 days of O' only having to give up ½ day of vacation to travel to the meet site. This was freestyle-O where one had to get all the controls on ones map in any order in the shortest time. Much fun. The next 2 days were regular O but we were unlucky enough to have the worst weather of the trip then. 2 inches of rain in 24 hours made it impossible for me with my glasses so after being off the map and...

Page 3

overtime, I decided not to try the 3rd day in such a heavy rain. As all can imagine, I was greatly disappointed not to be able to go.

Overall, a very enjoyable trip. Not just for the orienteering, but because we had the chance to again spend time with old friends whom we had met through O' and because we made new friends with whom we could visit in future. Orienteering—the family sport.

World Class Competition 1994

by Margie Bone

Cascade members Eric and Nicholas Bone had the honor of representing the US in major events in Europe this summer. Along with James Scarborough from the Bay Area and Eirik Moe, raised in Norway but a US citizen studying in the US, and Matt Scott, from DVOA, and Karen Williams, they competed in the Junior World Orienteering Championships near Gdansk in northern Poland. There was a 4.1 km Short Distance event; Nicholas had a time of 31 minutes, Eric 35 minutes. The 10.8 km Classic event, held in high humidity with temperatures of 100F, was won in 71:41. The top two finishers were Polish and third was an Finn. Eric placed 58th with a time of 90 minutes and James finished 78th in 93 minutes. (There were 148 competitors.) The US team finished 34th in the Relay. Interestingly, Russia took 1st and 2nd; Finnish teams were 3rd and 4th.

After orienteering more casually in Norway and Scotland, Eric returned to Norway and then Denmark for World Cup events. The usual top US team members weren't there, but second-string members Eric, Rick Oliver and Ian Smith put in respectable performances, Eric in the A heats, Rick and Ian in the B. The A course in Norway was 14.2 km with 870 meters of climb! In Denmark, the terrain was much flatter for a 19.0 km individual course and for a relay with 3 legs, each about 10.5 km. Eric felt his strongest performance was in the relay. He was 19th of 35 competitors at the first radio control but then made a couple of mistakes, finishing 30th of about 35, but his time was still under 6 minute kilometers (62:37 for 10.5 k).

All this international competition is bound to strengthen the Husky Orienteering Team as Nicholas will start at the UW as a freshman and Lisa Nordland, who will transfer from Bellingham to the UW this fall, will be honing her skills at the Student World Championships in Switzerland in September. The team should have an excellent shot at a third straight championship at the US intercollegiates in the spring of '95.

While Eric was a newcomer to this level of competition, Debbie Newell, who is a first-stringer and represented the US in the 1990 World Cup series and in the World Championships last fall will travel to Germany for World Cup competition next month. She will be accompanied by Linda Hood, who will make good use of her German language skills in serving as team leader, representing the team in meetings, making necessary arrangements and insuring that team members are well informed. Good luck Debbie!

Ramblings of a Wild Man

by Dave Lilly

I am continuing to look into Cougar mountain as a place to map. New trails are being made and continue to add to an already impressive trail system. Dave Tallent's acquisition of a Sony Global Positioning System device could help in fixing, accurately, major trail junctions and large point features. I am looking into getting electronic contour files from USGS.

We have had a slight problem with the SAMM membership list. It was destroyed in a recent disk crash. Kathy Trost, Membership Coordinator, has done a great job reconstructing it. We may, however, have a few members that have slipped through the cracks. If you are a SAMM member, signed up before July 1, 1994, and have not received a newsletter, please let Kathy know. If you are a member who has renewed since Jan. 1, 1994, she may not have that record either. The list is being purged of expired members, so please note the expiration date on the newsletter label to be sure it concurs with your records. If you have any questions concerning your membership status, please contact Kathy at 271-5508.

The club has decided to purchase a copy of O-CAD. We are only waiting now for the new Windows release. According to Bruce McAlister, the new release will be easier to use. It will be able to be used with a wide range of map and field work scales. Fred Veler is also looking into other program options.

At a recent SAMM club meeting it was reported that Jan Urban and Jana Urbanova have completed the re-field checking of Luther Burbank Park and Island Crest/Pioneer Park on Mercer Island. Tentatively we are thinking that Island Crest will be updated on the original ink mylars and Luther Burbank may be put on O-cad. We at SAMM are very fortunate to have such a dedicated and hard-working couple taking on such formidable tasks. The club owes both Jana and Jan a deep debt of gratitude.

Lessons Relearned

by Mike Schuh

Back in June or so, I was asked by another member of the Norwegian dance group to which I belong if I'd be interested in teaching folk dancing for a week or so at a Norwegian heritage camp being held at Trollhaugen Lodge. Sure, I said, and, with the support of the camp staff and the endorsement of the COC Executive Board, added a one day orienteering meet to the activities. With the help of Bill Cusworth and a few others, we hosted 32 teenagers (13-16) and about 20 general public (mostly Seattle area regulars, but also a couple of newcomers). I set three courses and, with a few extra and re-used control locations, had a total of five courses: a novice, a long novice for the campers that ended at a swimming hole in the gravel pit, an extension of this that came back to the start (as did all of...

Page 4

the others), and a short advanced that was a short-circuited version of the long advanced. The latter included a 2.7km long leg to control number 7 and, because the weather was bright and sunny with a promise of being very hot, it had water at two locations—controls 5 and 9.

For the most part, the meet went well, and everyone seemed to enjoy the courses that I set. There were a few problems, however, and all the organizational errors were made by me. At Trollhaugen I made several, including some that I haven't made ever before. I share them to urge upon everyone the importance of checking every last little detail, even at a "simple" local meet. The potential always exists for a real disaster, which I escaped it here not once, but twice.

The simplest one was to put the wrong control code on the description sheet for control number 3 on courses 4 and 5. The marker in the forest was 131; I wrote 133. Those on course 5 had the possibility of noting that there was another 133 later on the course, but no one did. Most assumed (correctly) that I had made an error, and continued on. Debbie reasoned that there could not legally be another marker so near to where this one was, Mark thought "it's gotta be the right one," and Marti just didn't worry about it. Dave Enger noticed the error but just kept on going, while Dave Tallent searched the entire area and came back to the control twice before continuing on. Would that I had been so thorough.

I also had the height of the boulder wrong—in my field notes I had written 1.5m (the correct height) but somehow wrote 1.7m on the description sheet (my field notes also had the correct control code of 131). Marti, abetted by Debbie, was somewhat vocal about this error, especially so because a nearby boulder was about 1.7m high, and, of course, she found that one, and wondered if the marker was supposed to be there. With the other boulder a different height and possessing the "wrong" marker, this is not an unreasonable question.

On to the next mistake. I hung control 6 on courses 4 and 5 about 100m away from where I had planned it to be. Coming from the road (I used my trusty Volvo to set many of the controls) and setting the marker by memory, I placed it on the first rockface I came to, neglecting to recall that there was another one (the correct one) further along. To add to the problems, the area around the correct location had been clear cut, and those who looked for the other rockface never found any rockface under all of the debris. If I had taken a map with me when I was setting the control, I might have noticed this. As it was, several of the early finishers claimed that the marker was missing. When I went out to hang a new one, I found that the original was indeed still there, and that Peter Golde and Ken Lew were looking for it in the correct location but finding nothing. I told them where it was (leading Peter to curse me with "no way!") and as I did so Dave Enger, passing nearby enroute to the correct location, was led directly to it. Later, back at the finish I looked at a map and realized my error. No point doing anything about it then, as the only two others on the course (Debbie Newell and Dave Tallent) were just a few minutes behind Dave Enger, although they each spent enough time looking in vain for it (and Dave Tallent for number 3) that I might have seen them had I gone back out (I was delayed somewhat in my return to the finish as I went to the campers' swimming hole to get their control cards).

Later, we estimated how much time each person lost at that control. Here are the "corrected" results:

(Robert took the road from number 5, which led him directly past the actual location of number 6. Dave uses his watch to time the duration of his difficulties, and tried to claim another 28:40 of correction due to control number 3—this was roundly rejected by the others present, most loudly by Debbie. Arne had taken his dog with him, and the dog got dehydrated, compelling Arne to quit early. Marti was so discouraged by not finding number 6 that she did not persist in finding number 8, which was slightly difficult. Ken didn't find number 1. Not finding number 6, Paul continued on around the rest of the course without punching; Bill's story is below.)

As I review these results, I am taken by how much a simple error was able to affect the outcome of the event. But the two problems with the markers were nothing compared to the other, more disturbing things that took place.

The first scare that I experienced actually happened after the second one. Because Bill Cusworth was helping with registration, he did not start until nearly everyone else had gone and come. He also left the start knowing where control number 6 really was, and the real control code at number 3. He started at 1:06; as he left I told him that I would begin picking up markers at 1:30 or 2:00. I got distracted in a number of other activities and did not make a start on retrieving markers.

By about 3:30, I began to wonder about Bill's whereabouts. At 4:30 I decided it was time to go look for him, as he was the only one left unaccounted for (or so I thought). Debbie Newell and Dave Tallent were still loitering about the finish (working on their suntans until it clouded over and then feeding mosquitoes), and they agreed to help out. Leaving Debbie at the finish, I sent Dave driving up the road towards Meany Lodge, in the general direction of the two-thirds point of the course. Meanwhile, I started to run the course backwards, agreeing to meet at a given spot on the road (near number 11). When I got there, having picked up a couple of markers enroute and calling for Bill all the...

Page 5

while (now there's a sobering activity!), I found Dave's car with Bill inside (Dave had left to pick up another marker). Bill was fine, and his story was interesting, though minor.

When he left number 6, he was doing really well and decided to push it a bit for the rest of the course. But as he neared the end of the 2.3km leg to number 7, he began to get severely dehydrated, having pushed himself a bit too much, and so headed for the water control at number 9. When he got there, he downed a gallon or so of water and waited for me to come get him (because we always look for those that are still out on the course and anyway I was going to begin picking up markers a half hour or so after he started, right?). Well, after a while he got tired of waiting and picked up the marker and water (thanks, Bill!) and started walking back down the road where Dave met him. The three of us picked up a few more markers and then headed back to the lodge.

It was after dinner that I got my second and much more serious scare. Somehow I had missed the fact that two of the campers never finished course 1. Earlier in the day I had started to run an out-count on course 1, and my notes (which I examined later that evening) did show the two girls to be missing, but I did not react to it at the time because there were a few cards the weren't posted. I did do a complete out-count on course 2, and verified that everyone who had started that course did make it to the swimming hole—but these two never started on course 2!

Instead they walked off the map. Really. They found control number 4, which was on the southern tip of a (dried up) marsh. But it was down off the road they were on and they chose to continue east on the road instead of crossing the marsh and following the powerline back west towards the finish. Two and a half dry, hot hours later they bumped into the Yakima River at the eastern edge of the map. Some campers on the other side convinced them to cross the turbulent river and they were given a ride back to the lodge, arriving about 2:30pm.

On Tuesday (two days later) I walked the route with them to look for a pair of glasses they'd lost, and tried to understand why, when they realized that they were lost, they didn't simply turn around and backtrack. They didn't know, and I am left wondering how much worse it might have been...

I also wonder what I could have done different. This was the first orienteering event for either of them. Perhaps I should have included backtracking in the talk I gave the night before. Certainly I should have set control number 4 in a better location. And absolutely I should been more diligent in checking who was still out on the course. But even if I had noticed at, say, noon that they were still out on course 1, what then? Running the course backwards would not have found them—they were a half mile or more away from the nearest marker and walking even farther away. I doubt that I would have found them before they got to the river, even if I had torn up the roads in my Volvo.

The lesson here is to be very careful when designing novice courses. Remember, this is often the first time the participants have been out in a forest, and we can't assume anything about their skills or abilities.

To recap: Double check your control descriptions. Always carry your map with you when setting out markers, no matter how good you think you are. Design your novice course with novices in mind—make it really easy. Teach raw beginners that backtracking can be a Very Good Idea. Be very, very diligent in checking who is still out on a course. And try to avoid making all of these mistakes at the same meet...

Orienteering Class

Mike Schuh will teach an introductory class on orienteering through the ASUW Experimental College. The class will be held at Discovery Park and will include outdoor exercises. For information regarding registration for the class, please call the Experimental College at 543-4375 (course number 4164, "Orienteering for Sport and Fun"). Mike may be reached at 783-3960. The class will begin Sunday, October 23rd, and will continue for a total of four Sundays. There will be two sections of two hours each: one beginning at 10:00am, the other at 12:30pm. Tuition $25, less for seniors, students.

The same class will be offered through the Lynnwood Parks Department on four consecutive Saturdays, beginning October 22nd, from 10:00am until noon. At Lynndale Park; $22. Information: 771-4030

These classes are excellent for novices, but also useful for those with a bit of experience who wish to get a solid background of the basics of orienteering. This is Mike's 14th year teaching these classes.

Class outline
I. Introduction, map symbols, what to do when lost, map walk
II. Map symbols review, imaginary map walk, line O' course.
III. Compass, pacing, compass course.
IV. Route choice, basic tactics, novice course, what next...

Hotline Improvements

There is a new machine handling calls for the hotline. It features four separate messages so we can have more complete information about each of four subjects without running out of time on the tape or frustrating listeners who want certain specific information such as driving directions to the next meet or whether there will be a meet while their guests are here from out-of-town. The initial message contains directions to the other three options, then goes on with information for newcomers. People can also leave a message at the end of message #1; those messages are checked perioically by Ardis who then mails out information packets.

Page 6

Message two has a brief rundown of the schedule for the next couple of months, with names and phone numbers of meet directors. Message three describes the next local event in detail and message four is about the next major event in the area. For example, the current message #4 is about the Short Course Champs and "A" meet in Cle Elum September 9th and 10th.

The hotline messages are now done exclusively by Margie Bone and Rick Hood; it would be an option for meet directors to do their own messages about their meets. If you are a meet director and would like to do this, call Margie or Rick for instructions. We are enthusiastic about the new capabilities of the hotline. Please feel free to call with any suggestions for how to get the best use out of it.

FOR SALE: Former hotline machine. Panasonic AutoLogic with fully remote operation. Was a little over $100 new about a year ago. Still works—we just wanted to upgrade. $40 or best offer. Call Margie at 634-2279.

Field Notes

by Rick Hood

Cle Elum Sept. 10/11
Don't miss this event, and really try to avoid the "late fee" deadline, so post your entry form with check by August 28. Call Don at 488-2070 if you have any questions or need a spare form. Also, don't forget to order your Debbie Newell-designed "US Short Course Championship T-shirt"; the word on the trail is they are really cool! Lastly, staying at Camp Koinonia is going to be the "place to be", what with hot showers and refreshing swimming pond and hearty food. (Saturday's banquet is limited to the first 125.) For more info, call the hotline and hit the number 4 key, which gets you to the major event section.

Trim Course at Woodland Park Awaits You Now
It has taken a while, but the permanent orienteering course is ready for your use at Woodland Park! (Thanks to Brad and Rick for repainting the markers.) There are three course options plus a freestyle approach. All courses start from the North end of the long bank of tennis courts. For those interested in getting in some practice, or even trying O for the first time, before the A meet, Margie (634-2279) will have maps for loan on Saturday September 3rd from 10am to noon. Otherwise, bring your own map and copy from the Master Map Info Board attached to the fence at the North end of the long bank of tennis courts, or buy a xerox map at "Super Jock 'n Jill."

Samm's First Trim Course Looks Fun
Sammamish OC is establishing a trim course at Robinswood Park in Bellevue. Most of the work was done for a Boy Scout Project by one of Samm's up and coming orienteers. Call Dave or Val at 822-6254 for more info.

Inland Empire Cup—A Hot One
A small part of the map has changed a bit since last year; some brush went up in flames! But the course setters have elected to turn this into a plus and have promised their usual great courses for the event. Like last year, this will be the Washington State Championships; so make the easy drive over (4 hours for Peter and 5.5 for the rest of us) for a super weekend October 1 and 2. [I could do it faster if it weren't for those slow VW vans on the road -Ed.] Oh, don't miss the event registration deadline or you will be marking your maps on the clock, instead of receiving one with your course and map corrections already indicated. All the details will be on the hot line in option 4 (the major event section) after September 13th.

Fort Casey and the 1994 Pig War
The pig war (the annual friendly rivalry that takes place at one Washington State and BC island site) will take place at Fort Casey on October 8 with the BC part the following weekend at Mt. Douglas. We friendly Washington State types have moved the start times to noon-2pm so as to make our BC friends feel both welcome and encouraged to join us for the day. Also, we are reviving the popular "drive your car to the ferry and bike from there to Ft. Casey". Check the hotline for details after October 3rd.

Vampire O IV
The annual vampire O, will be held at Hamlin Park in North Seattle on Sunday the 30th at 5:30 with a mass start at 6pm. (The clocks fall back that morning so it will be dark and spooky.) Remember to bring a good flashlight, your courage, and garlic (just kidding about the garlic, although we are going for pizza just down the street afterwards...) Don't miss this annual screamer, details on section 3 of the hotline after October 25.

Congratulations
John Rogers and Beth Eliot have a beautiful baby girl! Trish and Aaron Charlop's son is growing quickly, though not yet ready to teethe on punch cards yet. And special congrats to all the strong finishers at the North American Champs. Lastly, a well deserved "way-to-go" to Sammamish OC and especially course setter Mark Howlett, for their recent Ros*Elum event (Honorable mention to Mac for his dinner and beverage recommendations where some 20 orienteers showed up. The rumor about replacing punches at controls with darts for a biathlon event is not true at this time, to the best of my knowledge.)

Page 7

Travels
The Bones had a super trip orienteering and traveling in Scotland and Scandinavia...Harvey & Annie are still enjoying Europe...Rune Hoel is about to head home to Norway after completing his studies at the UW; we wish him the best and are grateful for the orienteering insights he shared...Bruce McAlister is an orienteering hot property for the upcoming US Relay Championships back East (he is worth 3 points, while Eric Bone is worth "nothing"; well, that isn't quite the case)...

Writing
Received a nice postcard from Peter Bonek and Claudia Kunzel, who hosted some nice clinics for us during their 92-94 stay while studying at UBC. Even More Cle-Elum?

As if the two-day "A" meet at Cle Elum Ridge weren't enough, Cascade is following up with another day of orienteering at Cle Elum Ridge the following weekend. This will be a long Score event, similar in format (but not terrain!) to the annual Rambo meet. There will be around 30-35 markers, with a time limit of between two and three hours (I haven't quite decided yet).

Most of the control locations will be ones used at the previous weekend, but since they will be picked roughly equally from the 16 courses, you will have seen only a minority of the controls. The maps will be pre-marked. Please call or e-mail Peter Golde (883-3732, petergo@microsoft.com) to pre-register to get a map and confirm that you are coming. If you don't register, you will have to copy your map on the clock.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

block_index.text: BEARING 315 The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest September, 1994

Three Seattle orienteers, Cascade members Anne York, Harvey Freidman and Margie Bone participated in the Veterans World Cup competition in Aviemore, Scotland August 1-5. First let me clarify that this has nothing to do with either the military or with amount of orienteering experience; a "veteran" is simply someone over 35 years of age. There were 3000 participants in the VWC and then additional participants, including Eric and Nicholas Bone and Margie's parents, on "colour-coded" courses with day of event registration for those not in the Veteran's Cup.

About fifteen people from the US were there, including Sherry Litasi who will direct the VWC event in Minneapolis in 1997. There were strong performances by Al Smith of A&E Orienteering Supply fame and by Penny DeMoss from Bay Area Orienteering Club. Penny placed 10th out of about 80 women in the W40 category. It turns out Penny and her husband Harold spend about 4 months each year living in Scotland, but she still calls BAOC her home club.

The terrain was varied, with both deciduous and evergreen forests, open grazing land and large areas of heather. I had thought of heather as a soft, small plant, but lovely as it is, it is a bush to be reckoned with. It is difficult to run through, particularly for shorter people on whom the heather came to their waists. (We didn't run through any but there are some areas where the heather is burned each year because a particular type of grouse lives primarily on new shoots of heather.) Many of the evergreen areas were farmed so there were irrigation ditches between the rows of trees, and running was a lot easier along the rows than across (this I learned when my route required my going several hundred meters across).

The courses were longer than what I am used to running and there were long walks to the starts some days. Qualifying days for the 40-44 year old women were about 5 kms with up to a 3 km walk to the start. The final day was 7.2 km with a 2.5 km walk to the start. No ladders though! (I understand that participants had to climb a ladder to get to the start for one of the courses in the Scottish 6-Day last year.)

Many people found qualifying day two to be the most difficult with two distinctly different types of terrain on a single map. One part had fairly large features, then the last half of the course was in a wooded area with an incredibly complex grouping of small hills and depressions. I was being kind of casual about choosing clearly identifiable attack points because the earlier part of the course had allowed successful use of compass and pace, sometimes for entire legs. Then I got into an area where all the hills and all the depressions were about the same size and shape as the others and it was hilly and brushy enough that pace counting was difficult. There were areas with lots of competitors milling around, asking people if they knew where we were. It was a good reminder to me to plan each leg in a disciplined way and to try to keep all my orienteering techniques in mind for each leg; the course setter did a wonderful job of tapping all those skills.

It was a beautiful area (except for the town of Aviemore itself which is very touristy) with good bed and breakfasts, miles and miles of stone fences, impressive castles (it's awe inspiring to explore buildings built in the 1300's and to see the room where Mary Queen of Scots gave birth to King James the VIth), friendly people and challenging orienteering. The same Strathspey area will be the site of the Scottish 6-Day next year. If anyone would like information about that, please feel free to ask me.

Copyright © 1994, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for free distribution for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club.

In case any of you wondered why Anne York & Harvey Friedman didn't attend any local meets from 15 July to 15 August 1994, we were orienteering at multi-day international events in Europe. 19-23 July we did Nordvest Galoppen '94 near Nordfjordeid and Stryn, Norway. 31 July—5 August we did Veterans' World Cup near Aviemore, Scotland. 7 August we did a 1-day event at Blakeley Raise in the Lakes District, England. 12-14 August we were at the Drammen 3-day, outside of Drammen, Norway. As those of you who have traveled in Europe well realize, hopping back and forth between Great Britain and Norway can be quite expensive. It is, but we didn't do it just for the terrific international competition. Orienteering is the true family sport suitable for all ages in these big European meets and for 10 years old and up everywhere else I've tried it. More than that, orienteering is still a sport where all participants are part of the orienteering family and big meets are one way to get together with distant "O' cousins".

In particular, Anne & I and the Bone family were met at the airport by Magne & Else Raadal (who were here last September-December ) in Bergen, Norway and helped with finding our rental car and a cash machine so we could be on our way north for NVG '94. The meet had about 700 people from about 12 different countries with most coming from western Norway. The format was run 2 days, 1 rest day, and run 2 more days with points being computed each run day. Total points for the best 3 out of 4 days determined the placings for each class. Of the 5 of us, only Eric Bone did consistently well. After the event, we drove back to Bergen through some spectacular scenery and took an hour long ferry ride so that the Bones could see even more and finally returned to the Raadal home for well-needed R&R. While in Bergen, Anne & I visited Stein Sandven, who some of you might remember from the 1981-1982 years. Stein has married Hanne Sagen, a graduate student working at Nansen Remote Sensing Center, and they have a 2-year-old son, Haakon. Buying and trying to fix up and older home and Haakon have curtailed Stein's O' activities recently but he expects to be back in 3 years. While at the Raadal's, Eric, Margie, and I competed in a 5 K fun run. Eric placed 7th overall and I racewalked it in about 35 minutes. Not bad for not having trained in RW in over a year.

The Bones left for Great Britain, where they would meet Margie's parents and drive up to Scotland and Anne & I had another relaxing day in Bergen. When we flew in to Newcastle, we had the misfortune of being in the midst of the BritRail strike, so our plan to take the train directly to Aviemore was put on hold for 2 days. (Anyone planning to get a BritRail pass or Flexipass, ask your agent about strikes. Rail strikes can play havoc with ones travel plans and budget.) We finally got to Aviemore where we were met at the rail station by David Booth & Jean Smith, a couple of British orienteers who we had met and befriended last summer at the Hamlin Park meet. We shared a wonderful, refurbished, stone cottage with them during VWC. It had a modern kitchen, 2 bedrooms, 2 bathrooms, etc.

VWC94 itself was on superb maps of intricate terrain with over 3000 competitors from more than 30 countries. What we hadn't quite realized when we had signed up for this event was that it is the age class world championships for those over 35. Consequently, many former world champions were there and overall these were the fittest, most successful orienteers in the world. As you might have guessed, those of us who were below average in rankings in the US didn't do very well against the best in the world.

One somewhat sad note to relate. One of the Swedish women who had been in O' for over 50 years and who ran in W75 died on her way to the 1st control on the final day. At first, we were dismayed but those people who had known her best said that many times she expressed the following sentiments: —I do not want to die of Alzheimer's disease. I do not want to die as a vegetable in a nursing home. I do not want to be sick and waste away in pain. If I am to die, I would prefer it to be running through the woods doing something I've loved for years.— So the rest of us all felt better thinking that she had died happily.

After VWC94, Anne & I drove down to Ulverston with Jean & David and went to a "color-coded event" in the Lakes District. In the moor lands I had my best run of the vacation, doing a 3.8 K course in under 56 minutes. It was similar to Marymoor Park in visibility and openness, but more like Cle Elum Ridge in steepness.

After spending a day being tourists in Ulverston, home of Swarthmoor Hall, where the Society of Friends (Quakers) was founded, we departed by train for Heathrow Airport, London and our flight to Oslo. Ingemar J. Haverstad, who some of you might remember when he was Ingemar Jansson, met us at the airport and took us home to Helga and the 3 kids. They have a wonderful house in Tranby on the edge of some wonderful hills with steep cliffs and full of cows, sheep, goats, berries, etc. Naturally, there is O' map of it. Anne, guided by the 6 year-old Sindre, took him and 4 year-old Eirik, up the hill to pick blueberries. Using the Scandinavian 'plokker', one of which Anee brought home, they picked about 5 liters in 2 hours. Anne introduced home-made blueberry pie to the gang and everyone wanted more. The next day we combined a hike, lake stay, O' training, with more blueberry picking and thus more pie. The 1st day of the 3-day had a mass start at 1800 to 1930, with 10 minutes separating each of the classes. In this way, folks could get 3 days of O' only having to give up ½ day of vacation to travel to the meet site. This was freestyle-O where one had to get all the controls on ones map in any order in the shortest time. Much fun. The next 2 days were regular O but we were unlucky enough to have the worst weather of the trip then. 2 inches of rain in 24 hours made it impossible for me with my glasses so after being off the map and...
overtime, I decided not to try the 3rd day in such a heavy rain. As all can imagine, I was greatly disappointed not to be able to go.

Overall, a very enjoyable trip. Not just for the orienteering, but because we had the chance to again spend time with old friends whom we had met through O' and because we made new friends with whom we could visit in future. Orienteering—the family sport.

Cascade members Eric and Nicholas Bone had the honor of representing the US in major events in Europe this summer. Along with James Scarborough from the Bay Area and Eirik Moe, raised in Norway but a US citizen studying in the US, and Matt Scott, from DVOA, and Karen Williams, they competed in the Junior World Orienteering Championships near Gdansk in northern Poland. There was a 4.1 km Short Distance event; Nicholas had a time of 31 minutes, Eric 35 minutes. The 10.8 km Classic event, held in high humidity with temperatures of 100F, was won in 71:41. The top two finishers were Polish and third was an Finn. Eric placed 58th with a time of 90 minutes and James finished 78th in 93 minutes. (There were 148 competitors.) The US team finished 34th in the Relay. Interestingly, Russia took 1st and 2nd; Finnish teams were 3rd and 4th.

After orienteering more casually in Norway and Scotland, Eric returned to Norway and then Denmark for World Cup events. The usual top US team members weren't there, but second-string members Eric, Rick Oliver and Ian Smith put in respectable performances, Eric in the A heats, Rick and Ian in the B. The A course in Norway was 14.2 km with 870 meters of climb! In Denmark, the terrain was much flatter for a 19.0 km individual course and for a relay with 3 legs, each about 10.5 km. Eric felt his strongest performance was in the relay. He was 19th of 35 competitors at the first radio control but then made a couple of mistakes, finishing 30th of about 35, but his time was still under 6 minute kilometers (62:37 for 10.5 k).

All this international competition is bound to strengthen the Husky Orienteering Team as Nicholas will start at the UW as a freshman and Lisa Nordland, who will transfer from Bellingham to the UW this fall, will be honing her skills at the Student World Championships in Switzerland in September. The team should have an excellent shot at a third straight championship at the US intercollegiates in the spring of '95.

While Eric was a newcomer to this level of competition, Debbie Newell, who is a first-stringer and represented the US in the 1990 World Cup series and in the World Championships last fall will travel to Germany for World Cup competition next month. She will be accompanied by Linda Hood, who will make good use of her German language skills in serving as team leader, representing the team in meetings, making necessary arrangements and insuring that team members are well informed. Good luck Debbie!

I am continuing to look into Cougar mountain as a place to map. New trails are being made and continue to add to an already impressive trail system. Dave Tallent's acquisition of a Sony Global Positioning System device could help in fixing, accurately, major trail junctions and large point features. I am looking into getting electronic contour files from USGS.

We have had a slight problem with the SAMM membership list. It was destroyed in a recent disk crash. Kathy Trost, Membership Coordinator, has done a great job reconstructing it. We may, however, have a few members that have slipped through the cracks. If you are a SAMM member, signed up before July 1, 1994, and have not received a newsletter, please let Kathy know. If you are a member who has renewed since Jan. 1, 1994, she may not have that record either. The list is being purged of expired members, so please note the expiration date on the newsletter label to be sure it concurs with your records. If you have any questions concerning your membership status, please contact Kathy at 271-5508.

The club has decided to purchase a copy of O-CAD. We are only waiting now for the new Windows release. According to Bruce McAlister, the new release will be easier to use. It will be able to be used with a wide range of map and field work scales. Fred Veler is also looking into other program options.

At a recent SAMM club meeting it was reported that Jan Urban and Jana Urbanova have completed the re-field checking of Luther Burbank Park and Island Crest/Pioneer Park on Mercer Island. Tentatively we are thinking that Island Crest will be updated on the original ink mylars and Luther Burbank may be put on O-cad. We at SAMM are very fortunate to have such a dedicated and hard-working couple taking on such formidable tasks. The club owes both Jana and Jan a deep debt of gratitude.

Back in June or so, I was asked by another member of the Norwegian dance group to which I belong if I'd be interested in teaching folk dancing for a week or so at a Norwegian heritage camp being held at Trollhaugen Lodge. Sure, I said, and, with the support of the camp staff and the endorsement of the COC Executive Board, added a one day orienteering meet to the activities. With the help of Bill Cusworth and a few others, we hosted 32 teenagers (13-16) and about 20 general public (mostly Seattle area regulars, but also a couple of newcomers). I set three courses and, with a few extra and re-used control locations, had a total of five courses: a novice, a long novice for the campers that ended at a swimming hole in the gravel pit, an extension of this that came back to the start (as did all of...

the others), and a short advanced that was a short-circuited version of the long advanced. The latter included a 2.7km long leg to control number 7 and, because the weather was bright and sunny with a promise of being very hot, it had water at two locations—controls 5 and 9.

For the most part, the meet went well, and everyone seemed to enjoy the courses that I set. There were a few problems, however, and all the organizational errors were made by me. At Trollhaugen I made several, including some that I haven't made ever before. I share them to urge upon everyone the importance of checking every last little detail, even at a "simple" local meet. The potential always exists for a real disaster, which I escaped it here not once, but twice.

The simplest one was to put the wrong control code on the description sheet for control number 3 on courses 4 and 5. The marker in the forest was 131; I wrote 133. Those on course 5 had the possibility of noting that there was another 133 later on the course, but no one did. Most assumed (correctly) that I had made an error, and continued on. Debbie reasoned that there could not legally be another marker so near to where this one was, Mark thought "it's gotta be the right one," and Marti just didn't worry about it. Dave Enger noticed the error but just kept on going, while Dave Tallent searched the entire area and came back to the control twice before continuing on. Would that I had been so thorough.

I also had the height of the boulder wrong—in my field notes I had written 1.5m (the correct height) but somehow wrote 1.7m on the description sheet (my field notes also had the correct control code of 131). Marti, abetted by Debbie, was somewhat vocal about this error, especially so because a nearby boulder was about 1.7m high, and, of course, she found that one, and wondered if the marker was supposed to be there. With the other boulder a different height and possessing the "wrong" marker, this is not an unreasonable question.

On to the next mistake. I hung control 6 on courses 4 and 5 about 100m away from where I had planned it to be. Coming from the road (I used my trusty Volvo to set many of the controls) and setting the marker by memory, I placed it on the first rockface I came to, neglecting to recall that there was another one (the correct one) further along. To add to the problems, the area around the correct location had been clear cut, and those who looked for the other rockface never found any rockface under all of the debris. If I had taken a map with me when I was setting the control, I might have noticed this. As it was, several of the early finishers claimed that the marker was missing. When I went out to hang a new one, I found that the original was indeed still there, and that Peter Golde and Ken Lew were looking for it in the correct location but finding nothing. I told them where it was (leading Peter to curse me with "no way!") and as I did so Dave Enger, passing nearby enroute to the correct location, was led directly to it. Later, back at the finish I looked at a map and realized my error. No point doing anything about it then, as the only two others on the course (Debbie Newell and Dave Tallent) were just a few minutes behind Dave Enger, although they each spent enough time looking in vain for it (and Dave Tallent for number 3) that I might have seen them had I gone back out (I was delayed somewhat in my return to the finish as I went to the campers' swimming hole to get their control cards).
Later, we estimated how much time each person lost at that control. Here are the "corrected" results:

(Robert took the road from number 5, which led him directly past the actual location of number 6. Dave uses his watch to time the duration of his difficulties, and tried to claim another 28:40 of correction due to control number 3—this was roundly rejected by the others present, most loudly by Debbie. Arne had taken his dog with him, and the dog got dehydrated, compelling Arne to quit early. Marti was so discouraged by not finding number 6 that she did not persist in finding number 8, which was slightly difficult. Ken didn't find number 1. Not finding number 6, Paul continued on around the rest of the course without punching; Bill's story is below.)

As I review these results, I am taken by how much a simple error was able to affect the outcome of the event. But the two problems with the markers were nothing compared to the other, more disturbing things that took place.

The first scare that I experienced actually happened after the second one. Because Bill Cusworth was helping with registration, he did not start until nearly everyone else had gone and come. He also left the start knowing where control number 6 really was, and the real control code at number 3. He started at 1:06; as he left I told him that I would begin picking up markers at 1:30 or 2:00. I got distracted in a number of other activities and did not make a start on retrieving markers.

By about 3:30, I began to wonder about Bill's whereabouts. At 4:30 I decided it was time to go look for him, as he was the only one left unaccounted for (or so I thought). Debbie Newell and Dave Tallent were still loitering about the finish (working on their suntans until it clouded over and then feeding mosquitoes), and they agreed to help out. Leaving Debbie at the finish, I sent Dave driving up the road towards Meany Lodge, in the general direction of the two-thirds point of the course. Meanwhile, I started to run the course backwards, agreeing to meet at a given spot on the road (near number 11). When I got there, having picked up a couple of markers enroute and calling for Bill all the...
while (now there's a sobering activity!), I found Dave's car with Bill inside (Dave had left to pick up another marker). Bill was fine, and his story was interesting, though minor.

When he left number 6, he was doing really well and decided to push it a bit for the rest of the course. But as he neared the end of the 2.3km leg to number 7, he began to get severely dehydrated, having pushed himself a bit too much, and so headed for the water control at number 9. When he got there, he downed a gallon or so of water and waited for me to come get him (because we always look for those that are still out on the course and anyway I was going to begin picking up markers a half hour or so after he started, right?). Well, after a while he got tired of waiting and picked up the marker and water (thanks, Bill!) and started walking back down the road where Dave met him. The three of us picked up a few more markers and then headed back to the lodge.

It was after dinner that I got my second and much more serious scare. Somehow I had missed the fact that two of the campers never finished course 1. Earlier in the day I had started to run an out-count on course 1, and my notes (which I examined later that evening) did show the two girls to be missing, but I did not react to it at the time because there were a few cards the weren't posted. I did do a complete out-count on course 2, and verified that everyone who had started that course did make it to the swimming hole—but these two never started on course 2!

Instead they walked off the map. Really. They found control number 4, which was on the southern tip of a (dried up) marsh. But it was down off the road they were on and they chose to continue east on the road instead of crossing the marsh and following the powerline back west towards the finish. Two and a half dry, hot hours later they bumped into the Yakima River at the eastern edge of the map. Some campers on the other side convinced them to cross the turbulent river and they were given a ride back to the lodge, arriving about 2:30pm.

On Tuesday (two days later) I walked the route with them to look for a pair of glasses they'd lost, and tried to understand why, when they realized that they were lost, they didn't simply turn around and backtrack. They didn't know, and I am left wondering how much worse it might have been...

I also wonder what I could have done different. This was the first orienteering event for either of them. Perhaps I should have included backtracking in the talk I gave the night before. Certainly I should have set control number 4 in a better location. And absolutely I should been more diligent in checking who was still out on the course. But even if I had noticed at, say, noon that they were still out on course 1, what then? Running the course backwards would not have found them—they were a half mile or more away from the nearest marker and walking even farther away. I doubt that I would have found them before they got to the river, even if I had torn up the roads in my Volvo.

The lesson here is to be very careful when designing novice courses. Remember, this is often the first time the participants have been out in a forest, and we can't assume anything about their skills or abilities.

To recap: Double check your control descriptions. Always carry your map with you when setting out markers, no matter how good you think you are. Design your novice course with novices in mind—make it really easy. Teach raw beginners that backtracking can be a Very Good Idea. Be very, very diligent in checking who is still out on a course. And try to avoid making all of these mistakes at the same meet...

Mike Schuh will teach an introductory class on orienteering through the ASUW Experimental College. The class will be held at Discovery Park and will include outdoor exercises. For information regarding registration for the class, please call the Experimental College at 543-4375 (course number 4164, "Orienteering for Sport and Fun"). Mike may be reached at 783-3960. The class will begin Sunday, October 23rd, and will continue for a total of four Sundays. There will be two sections of two hours each: one beginning at 10:00am, the other at 12:30pm. Tuition $25, less for seniors, students.
The same class will be offered through the Lynnwood Parks Department on four consecutive Saturdays, beginning October 22nd, from 10:00am until noon. At Lynndale Park; $22. Information: 771-4030

These classes are excellent for novices, but also useful for those with a bit of experience who wish to get a solid background of the basics of orienteering. This is Mike's 14th year teaching these classes.

Class outline I. Introduction, map symbols, what to do when lost, map walk II. Map symbols review, imaginary map walk, line O' course. III. Compass, pacing, compass course. IV. Route choice, basic tactics, novice course, what next...

There is a new machine handling calls for the hotline. It features four separate messages so we can have more complete information about each of four subjects without running out of time on the tape or frustrating listeners who want certain specific information such as driving directions to the next meet or whether there will be a meet while their guests are here from out-of-town. The initial message contains directions to the other three options, then goes on with information for newcomers. People can also leave a message at the end of message #1; those messages are checked perioically by Ardis who then mails out information packets.

Message two has a brief rundown of the schedule for the next couple of months, with names and phone numbers of meet directors. Message three describes the next local event in detail and message four is about the next major event in the area. For example, the current message #4 is about the Short Course Champs and "A" meet in Cle Elum September 9th and 10th.

The hotline messages are now done exclusively by Margie Bone and Rick Hood; it would be an option for meet directors to do their own messages about their meets. If you are a meet director and would like to do this, call Margie or Rick for instructions. We are enthusiastic about the new capabilities of the hotline. Please feel free to call with any suggestions for how to get the best use out of it.

FOR SALE: Former hotline machine. Panasonic AutoLogic with fully remote operation. Was a little over $100 new about a year ago. Still works—we just wanted to upgrade. $40 or best offer. Call Margie at 634-2279.

Cle Elum Sept. 10/11 Don't miss this event, and really try to avoid the "late fee" deadline, so post your entry form with check by August 28. Call Don at 488-2070 if you have any questions or need a spare form. Also, don't forget to order your Debbie Newell-designed "US Short Course Championship T-shirt"; the word on the trail is they are really cool! Lastly, staying at Camp Koinonia is going to be the "place to be", what with hot showers and refreshing swimming pond and hearty food. (Saturday's banquet is limited to the first 125.) For more info, call the hotline and hit the number 4 key, which gets you to the major event section.

Trim Course at Woodland Park Awaits You Now It has taken a while, but the permanent orienteering course is ready for your use at Woodland Park! (Thanks to Brad and Rick for repainting the markers.) There are three course options plus a freestyle approach. All courses start from the North end of the long bank of tennis courts. For those interested in getting in some practice, or even trying O for the first time, before the A meet, Margie (634-2279) will have maps for loan on Saturday September 3rd from 10am to noon. Otherwise, bring your own map and copy from the Master Map Info Board attached to the fence at the North end of the long bank of tennis courts, or buy a xerox map at "Super Jock 'n Jill."

Samm's First Trim Course Looks Fun Sammamish OC is establishing a trim course at Robinswood Park in Bellevue. Most of the work was done for a Boy Scout Project by one of Samm's up and coming orienteers. Call Dave or Val at 822-6254 for more info.

Inland Empire Cup—A Hot One A small part of the map has changed a bit since last year; some brush went up in flames! But the course setters have elected to turn this into a plus and have promised their usual great courses for the event. Like last year, this will be the Washington State Championships; so make the easy drive over (4 hours for Peter and 5.5 for the rest of us) for a super weekend October 1 and 2. [I could do it faster if it weren't for those slow VW vans on the road -Ed.] Oh, don't miss the event registration deadline or you will be marking your maps on the clock, instead of receiving one with your course and map corrections already indicated. All the details will be on the hot line in option 4 (the major event section) after September 13th.

Fort Casey and the 1994 Pig War The pig war (the annual friendly rivalry that takes place at one Washington State and BC island site) will take place at Fort Casey on October 8 with the BC part the following weekend at Mt. Douglas. We friendly Washington State types have moved the start times to noon-2pm so as to make our BC friends feel both welcome and encouraged to join us for the day. Also, we are reviving the popular "drive your car to the ferry and bike from there to Ft. Casey". Check the hotline for details after October 3rd.

Vampire O IV The annual vampire O, will be held at Hamlin Park in North Seattle on Sunday the 30th at 5:30 with a mass start at 6pm. (The clocks fall back that morning so it will be dark and spooky.) Remember to bring a good flashlight, your courage, and garlic (just kidding about the garlic, although we are going for pizza just down the street afterwards...) Don't miss this annual screamer, details on section 3 of the hotline after October 25.

As if the two-day "A" meet at Cle Elum Ridge weren't enough, Cascade is following up with another day of orienteering at Cle Elum Ridge the following weekend. This will be a long Score event, similar in format (but not terrain!) to the annual Rambo meet. There will be around 30-35 markers, with a time limit of between two and three hours (I haven't quite decided yet).

Most of the control locations will be ones used at the previous weekend, but since they will be picked roughly equally from the 16 courses, you will have seen only a minority of the controls. The maps will be pre-marked. Please call or e-mail Peter Golde (883-3732, petergo@microsoft.com) to pre-register to get a map and confirm that you are coming. If you don't register, you will have to copy your map on the clock.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
